text: Hello and thanks for watching. Today I'm going to post just a straightforward robotic tap inguinal hernia repair. Mostly for patient education purposes.
The surgeon examines the left inguinal region, noting the presence of clips from a previous laparoscopic hernia repair. The mesh from the prior repair is faintly visible through the peritoneum.
The surgeon identifies a direct inguinal hernia defect on the right side, with no indirect inguinal hernia present. The peritoneal flap is prepared for the next stage of the procedure.
The surgeon creates a large peritoneal flap, a thin layer lining the abdominal cavity, and proceeds to reduce the hernia sac and its contents.

The camera view is oriented towards the right groin from above the belly button. The inferior epigastric blood vessels are visible at the top of the screen, leading down to the right groin. The hernia defect is identified based on its position relative to these vessels, with a direct hernia located medially and an indirect hernia laterally.
The video shows a young, healthy, and active male patient with a direct inguinal hernia on the right side.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the peritoneal flap on the right side, revealing the gonadal vessels. These vessels are visible extending from the bottom to the top of the screen, passing through the inguinal canal to supply blood to the right testicle.
A white tubular structure, identified as the vas deferens, is visible on the right side of the screen, entering the inguinal canal from a different direction than the gonadal vessels.
The surgeon reduces the hernia contents, revealing a small direct defect containing a large amount of incarcerated fat. The hernia sac is carefully manipulated to release the trapped tissue.
The surgeon observes preperitoneal fat tightly incarcerated within the hernia defect, noting its scarred appearance, which suggests it has been present for an extended period.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to gently tease and release scarred tissues, allowing the hernia contents to be reduced back into the abdomen. The final separation of scar tissue is completed, successfully reducing the hernia.

The surgeon continues the dissection behind the pubic bone on the right side, carefully working in front of the bladder, which is visible below the instruments. A space is being created to lay prosthetic mesh over the hernia defect and other areas of the groin susceptible to hernias.
The high resolution three-dimensional image with the robotic instrumentation is just incredible. I was never able to see this kind of detail doing it laparoscopically.
The surgeon reduces a small cord lipoma along the spermatic cord during the inguinal hernia operation, noting its size and potential impact on the patient.
The surgeon completes the dissection, ensuring the space is adequately enlarged around the hernia defect. The hernia defect is visible, and preparations are made to place the prosthetic mesh over it.
The surgeon places a polypropylene mesh, designed to contour to the groin, flat against the anatomical structures in the cavity. The mesh is fixated in at least three locations to prevent shifting.
The robotic instruments are used to place three sutures to fixate the polypropylene mesh in the groin area, ensuring it remains flat and in the correct position over the hernia defect.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap, ensuring the mesh is excluded from the abdominal cavity and not in contact with the bowel.

I'm going to jump forward here a little bit. This is a very lightly edited robotic inguinal hernia surgery. I believe this case took about fifty minutes for the entire operation. This gentleman went home the same day.

The surgeon shows the final product of the robotic inguinal hernia surgery, with the peritoneal cavity closed and the hernia sac visible. The surgeon pushes out CO₂ from the pocket to ensure the polypropylene mesh lays flat against the structures in the cavity.
